candidate_problems.text: If strong computational irreducibility holds, what kind of evidence can ever legitimately update P(doom) downward before full-scale deployment? The conjecture says only trajectory-level structural evidence counts, but this raises an unresolved epistemic problem: how much unfolding is enough to distinguish genuinely safe attractors from merely delayed failure modes, and is there any principled stopping rule short of surviving the most dangerous regimes?

Is dangerous agency actually an attractor of advanced AI architectures and deployment ecologies, or can corrigibility and bounded agency be made generative invariants of the system? This is the core ontological and mechanistic tension in the conjecture: whether power-seeking emerges from deep structural features of optimization, interaction, and competition, or whether alternative design/training/institutional stacks can reliably produce different long-run dynamics.

How can one identify and engineer sociotechnical feedback loops that remain stabilizing under capability scaling and competitive pressure? The conjecture relies on 'deployment ecology' as part of the generative structure, but leaves open which institutional arrangements, monitoring regimes, liability structures, or coordination mechanisms actually prevent safety margins from being eroded over open-ended trajectories.
